questions.explain: From $ijk=-1$ we get $ij=-k^{-1}=k$ (since $k^{-1}=-k$). Multiplying $ij=k$ on the left by $j^{-1}=-j$ and on the right by $i^{-1}=-i$ gives $ji=-k$. The quaternions are non-commutative: $ij=-ji$.
questions.hint: Multiply $ijk=-1$ on the right by $k^{-1}=-k$ to get $ij=k$, then use anticommutativity of $i,j$.
Sum the squares of the four real components.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes $\mathbb{H}$ as an algebra over $\mathbb{R}$?
choices: A 4-dimensional commutative ring with zero divisors
questions.explain: $\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^4$ is associative (matrix multiplication realises it), non-commutative ($ij=-ji$), and a division algebra (every non-zero $q$ has $q^{-1}=\overline q/|q|^2$). It is the unique such object beyond $\mathbb{R}$ and $\mathbb{C}$ (Frobenius).
questions.hint: Count basis elements and check whether $i,j,k$ commute.

questions.q: Which manifold is the group of unit quaternions diffeomorphic to?
questions.explain: Unit quaternions are $\{(a,b,c,d):a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2=1\}\subset\mathbb{R}^4$, which is exactly $S^3$. As a Lie group this is $\mathrm{Sp}(1)\cong\mathrm{SU}(2)$. Quotienting by $\{\pm 1\}$ gives $\mathrm{SO}(3)\cong\mathbb{RP}^3$.

questions.q: What is the kernel of the conjugation map $\mathrm{SU}(2)\to\mathrm{SO}(3),\ q\mapsto(v\mapsto qvq^{-1})$ acting on $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}\cong\mathbb{R}^3$?
choices: Trivial — the map is an isomorphism
questions.explain: $qvq^{-1}=v$ for every imaginary $v$ forces $q$ to commute with all of $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$, so $q\in\mathbb{R}\cap S^3=\{\pm 1\}$. Thus $\mathrm{SO}(3)\cong\mathrm{SU}(2)/\{\pm 1\}$ — the famous double cover.
questions.hint: $q$ must commute with $i,j,k$; the centre of $\mathbb{H}$ is $\mathbb{R}$.

questions.q: If $q=\cos(\theta/2)+\sin(\theta/2)\,\hat n$ with $\hat n\in\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$ a unit imaginary, what does the map $v\mapsto qvq^{-1}$ do to $\mathbb{R}^3=\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$?
questions.explain: Conjugation by a unit quaternion with half-angle $\theta/2$ rotates $\mathrm{Im}\,\mathbb{H}$ by the full angle $\theta$ around the axis $\hat n$. The half-angle is the source of the double cover: $q$ and $-q$ implement the same rotation.
questions.hint: Why is the cover 2-to-1? The half-angle in $q$ doubles when you conjugate.

questions.q: Going up the tower $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{O}$, which property is lost at the step $\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}$?
questions.explain: At each doubling something is sacrificed. $\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{C}$ loses $x^*=x$. $\mathbb{C}\to\mathbb{H}$ loses commutativity ($ij\neq ji$). $\mathbb{H}\to\mathbb{O}$ loses associativity. $\mathbb{O}\to\mathbb{S}$ (sedenions) loses the no-zero-divisor property.
questions.hint: Which of $i,j$ in $\mathbb{H}$ commute? They don't.

questions.q: What is the real dimension of the algebra produced by 5 iterations of Cayley–Dickson starting at $\mathbb{R}$ (so $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O},\mathbb{S},\dots$ — the 5th doubling)?
questions.explain: Each step doubles dimension: $1\to 2\to 4\to 8\to 16\to 32$. After $\mathbb{O}$ comes the sedenions $\mathbb{S}$ (dim 16); the 5th doubling gives the 32-dimensional 'trigintaduonions'. From $\mathbb{S}$ on, zero divisors appear.

questions.q: In Cayley–Dickson $(a,b)(c,d)=(ac-d^*b,da+bc^*)$ starting from $A=\mathbb{R}$ (so $a^*=a$), what algebra does the first doubling produce?
questions.explain: Setting $i=(0,1)$, the formula gives $i^2=(0,1)(0,1)=(-1\cdot 1,\ 0)=-1$. The doubling of $\mathbb{R}$ is $\mathbb{C}$, with conjugation $(a,b)^*=(a,-b)$ recovering the usual complex conjugate.

questions.q: Which identity holds in $\mathbb{O}$ but is strictly weaker than associativity?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{O}$ is alternative: any subalgebra generated by two elements is associative, so $x(xy)=(xx)y$ and similar identities hold even though general triples can fail to associate. Artin's theorem: alternative $\Leftrightarrow$ every two-generated subalgebra is associative.
$\mathbb{O}$ is the Cayley–Dickson double of $\mathbb{H}$, so $\dim_{\mathbb R}\mathbb{O}=2\cdot 4=8$, with basis $\{1,e_1,\dots,e_7\}$.

questions.q: How many copies of $\mathbb{H}$ sit inside $\mathbb{O}$ as 4-dimensional associative subalgebras containing $1$?
questions.explain: The 7 oriented lines of the Fano plane on $\{e_1,\dots,e_7\}$ each pick out a triple $\{e_a,e_b,e_c\}$ closed under multiplication, generating with $1$ a quaternion subalgebra $\mathbb{H}\subset\mathbb{O}$. So there are exactly 7 such copies of $\mathbb{H}$.
questions.hint: Count the lines of the Fano plane.

questions.q: What is the exact statement of Frobenius's theorem (1877)?
choices: Every commutative real algebra is $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$.
The only finite-dimensional associative division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$ are $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{C}$, $\mathbb{H}$.
The only normed real algebras are $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$.
Every finite field extension of $\mathbb{R}$ has degree $\le 2$.
questions.explain: Frobenius classifies finite-dimensional $\textbf{associative}$ division algebras over $\mathbb{R}$: exactly $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H}$. Dropping associativity admits $\mathbb{O}$ (Hurwitz, choice 3); finite extension fields of $\mathbb{R}$ are $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{C}$ (choice 4 is true but is the algebraic-closure fact, not Frobenius).

questions.q: Why does Frobenius's argument force the dimension of an associative real division algebra to be $1$, $2$, or $4$?
choices: Every real division algebra has a unique generator.
The orthogonal complement of $\mathbb{R}$ is a Euclidean space, and one can only fit $0$, $1$, or $3$ pairwise-anticommuting unit vectors there forced by associativity.
Real Banach algebras are at most $4$-dimensional.
questions.explain: Pick $x\notin\mathbb R$; its minimal polynomial is irreducible of degree $\le 2$, so $x^2\in\mathbb R$. The pure-imaginary part of the algebra is Euclidean. Associativity plus unit-norm anticommutativity force exactly $0$, $1$, or $3$ generators ($\mathbb R,\mathbb C,\mathbb H$); a fourth would violate $i(jk)=(ij)k$.
questions.hint: After a third generator $i,j,k$, where could a fourth go without breaking associativity?

questions.q: Which of these is $\textbf{not}$ a finite-dimensional associative real division algebra?
questions.explain: $\mathbb{O}$ is a division algebra but is non-associative, so it is excluded by Frobenius (which assumes associativity). It does appear in Hurwitz's broader classification of composition algebras.

questions.q: Hurwitz's theorem (1898) classifies which class of real algebras?
choices: Composition algebras (unital algebras with a quadratic norm satisfying $|xy|=|x||y|$)
Lie algebras with non-degenerate Killing form
questions.explain: Hurwitz: the only finite-dimensional real composition algebras are $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$. The $\textbf{norm-multiplicative}$ condition $|xy|=|x||y|$ is the key — it forces dimension $\in\{1,2,4,8\}$.

questions.q: How many distinct finite-dimensional real $\textbf{composition}$ algebras are there, up to isomorphism (Hurwitz)?
questions.explain: Exactly four: $\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C},\mathbb{H},\mathbb{O}$, of dimensions $1,2,4,8$. The dimension restriction matches the Bott–Milnor/Kervaire result that $\mathbb{R}^n$ admits a continuous bilinear product without zero divisors only for $n\in\{1,2,4,8\}$.

questions.q: The classical identity $(a^2+b^2+c^2+d^2)(e^2+f^2+g^2+h^2)=$ (sum of four squares) is an instance of which structure?
choices: The norm being multiplicative on $\mathbb{H}$ (Euler's four-square identity)
questions.explain: Euler's four-square identity is exactly $|q|^2|q'|^2=|qq'|^2$ for $q,q'\in\mathbb{H}$ — the multiplicativity of the quaternion norm. The eight-square identity (Degen) similarly comes from $\mathbb{O}$. There is no $16$-square identity, matching the failure of Cayley–Dickson at the sedenion step.
